Summary: The webpage discusses the "Give Big Kern 2026" initiative, which aims to raise funds and volunteer hours for local non-profit organizations in Kern County through online crowdfunding and matching donations. The article highlights the official launch of the early donation period, mentioning various participating organizations such as the American Cancer Association and Special Olympics Southern California. It also notes that in 2025, Give Big Kern raised over $1.7 million for 134 non-profits, and as of the article's writing, over $36,000 has been donated this year. The initiative connects charitable organizations with donors for causes like animal welfare, arts, education, and human services, with prizes awarded to top fundraisers.

The webpage announces the 2026 Victim Rights Event hosted by Kern County District Attorney Cynthia Zimmer on April 23, 2026. This event commemorates National Crime Victims' Rights Week and will honor victims of violent crime at the Violent Crime Victims Memorial. The event aims to raise awareness about victims' rights and services, featuring guest speakers, a tribute ceremony, and a march. It is free and open to the public, with a focus on supporting victims and their families, particularly within the Hispanic community.

The main topic of the webpage is an announcement about a partnership between Los Defensores and boxing legend Julio César Chávez. This collaboration aims to support the Hispanic community in the US, particularly in legal matters after accidents. Key terms include "Los Defensores," "Julio César Chávez," "Hispanic community," and "legal assistance." The article highlights the shared values of resilience and fighting for what's right between Chávez and Los Defensores, emphasizing their commitment to the Latino community.

The webpage discusses a candidate forum for California governor held in Fresno, focusing on issues relevant to the Central Valley. Key topics included affordable housing, immigrant community protection, environmental concerns, and unemployment rates. Candidates like Xavier Becerra, Matt Mahan, Kate Porter, Tony Thurmond, and Antonio Villaraigosa shared their plans. The article highlights the importance of supporting working-class and immigrant families, as well as the future of California's workforce.

The Boys & Girls Clubs of Kern County is recruiting intern sites for their Youth Summer Jobs Program. This program offers workforce readiness training to 300 local teens (ages 15-18) and selects the top 50 for paid summer internships. Businesses benefit from pre-screened, work-ready interns at no cost, as the Boys & Girls Clubs covers all expenses. The program aims to provide job training and experience for youth and businesses in Kern County.

The article discusses a co-ed 5k run against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) organized by Luz Almanza, founder of Girls Run Bakersfield. The event aims to create a safe community space for people to voice their concerns about immigration policies and treatment. Almanza, an immigrant herself, is passionate about social work and advocating for policy changes that provide legal status to long-term residents without fear of deportation. The run is personal to her experience and her desire to foster community, which she also pursues through her social work studies and the Girls Run Club.

The webpage discusses how the Mesa Verde ICE Processing Center restricts visitations during protests, a practice that has been ongoing for years. Advocates and community members express concern that this policy prevents families, some traveling long distances, from visiting their loved ones. The article highlights that Mesa Verde, a private facility, has a policy of temporarily closing visitation during protests, which raises questions about balancing security with family access. It also mentions that fear of detention deters some undocumented family members from visiting, and that attorneys have also experienced access restrictions.

The ACLU is defending a court order that prohibits illegal detention and stop practices by the Border Patrol. This order stems from a lawsuit filed by the United Farm Workers and five Kern County residents against the federal government after the Border Patrol allegedly detained and arrested Latinos, farmworkers, and day laborers without reasonable suspicion. The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals is being asked to uphold the preliminary injunction, which applies to operations in California's Eastern District. The article highlights concerns that the Border Patrol has violated constitutional rights and continues to do so, impacting working people and Latino communities.

El Popular, a Spanish-language publication in Kern County, is highlighting its weekly newsletter subscription. Key topics include the Give Big Kern 2026 donation campaign, local news like victim rights events and a candidate forum, and national news such as ACLU defenses and sports. The publication also touches on entertainment and economic impacts, mentioning the Hispanic community, immigrants, and truckers.
